examples.title: Algorithm: Test-and-Set Mutual Exclusion
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating atomic test_and_set instruction for mutual exclusion in critical section problem
examples.code:
ALGORITHM TestAndSet Mutual Exclusion
INPUT: shared lock (initially FALSE), process id i
OUTPUT: Process enters critical section

// Shared variables
VAR lock : BOOLEAN := FALSE

// === ATOMIC TEST_AND_SET OPERATION ===
FUNCTION test_and_set(target : POINTER TO BOOLEAN) : BOOLEAN
BEGIN
 boolean rv := *target // Save old value
 *target := TRUE // Set to TRUE atomically
 RETURN rv // Return old value
END FUNCTION

// === PROCESS Pi ENTERS CRITICAL SECTION ===
PROCEDURE Process_i()
BEGIN
 REPEAT
 // Busy wait until lock is FREE (test_and_set returns FALSE)
 WHILE test_and_set(&lock) = TRUE
 ; // Do nothing - spin
 END WHILE

 // === CRITICAL SECTION ===
 // Only one process can be here at a time
 
 lock := FALSE // Release the lock
 // === REMAINDER SECTION ===
 
 UNTIL FALSE
END PROCEDURE

// === BOUNDED WAITING VERSION ===
ALGORITHM BoundedWaitingTAS
INPUT: n processes, process index i
OUTPUT: Bounded waiting guarantee

VAR lock : BOOLEAN := FALSE
VAR waiting : ARRAY[0..n-1] OF BOOLEAN := {FALSE}

PROCEDURE Process_i(i)
BEGIN
 REPEAT
 waiting[i] := TRUE
 boolean key := TRUE

 WHILE waiting[i] = TRUE AND key = TRUE
 key := test_and_set(&lock)
 END WHILE

 waiting[i] := FALSE

 // === CRITICAL SECTION ===

 // Find next waiting process (circular)
 int j := (i + 1) MOD n
 WHILE j ≠ i AND waiting[j] = FALSE
 j := (j + 1) MOD n
 END WHILE

 IF j = i THEN
 lock := FALSE
 ELSE
 waiting[j] := FALSE
 END IF
 
 UNTIL FALSE
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: The test_and_set instruction atomically reads a value, sets it to TRUE, and returns the old value. In the mutual exclusion algorithm, a process spins in a while loop until test_and_set returns FALSE (meaning the lock was free). The bounded-waiting version adds a waiting array and circular scanning to ensure each process waits at most n-1 turns before entering the critical section.

examples.title: Python: Test-and-Set Spinlock Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating atomic test-and-set instruction for mutual exclusion with spinlock
examples.code:
import threading
import time

# Simulated hardware atomic test_and_set using Python's threading.Lock
# In real hardware, this would be a single CPU instruction
class TestAndSetLock:
 """
 Simulates atomic test_and_set instruction.
 In practice, this would use __sync_lock_test_and_set() or similar
 """
 def __init__(self):
 self._lock = threading.Lock() # For thread safety simulation
 self.value = False
 
 def test_and_set(self):
 """
 Atomically: old = *target; *target = TRUE; return old;
 This is a simulation - real hardware does this in one instruction
 """
 with self._lock:
 old_value = self.value
 self.value = True
 return old_value
 
 def release(self):
 """Release the lock"""
 self.value = False


class Spinlock:
 """Spinlock implementation using test-and-set concept"""
 def __init__(self):
 self.lock = TestAndSetLock()
 
 def acquire(self):
 """Spin until lock is acquired"""
 while self.lock.test_and_set():
 pass # Busy wait - spin
 
 def release(self):
 """Release the lock"""
 self.lock.release()
 
 def __enter__(self):
 self.acquire()
 return self
 
 def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
 self.release()


# Demo: Using spinlock in multi-threaded environment
shared_counter = 0
spinlock = Spinlock()

def critical_section_example(thread_id):
 """Example critical section using spinlock"""
 global shared_counter
 
 # Acquire lock before entering critical section
 spinlock.acquire()
 
 # === CRITICAL SECTION START ===
 print(f"Thread {thread_id} entered critical section")
 temp = shared_counter
 time.sleep(0.01) # Simulate work
 shared_counter = temp + 1
 print(f"Thread {thread_id} leaving critical section, counter={shared_counter}")
 # === CRITICAL SECTION END ===
 
 # Release lock
 spinlock.release()


# Test the spinlock
if __name__ == "__main__":
 threads = []
 
 # Create 5 threads that all try to increment counter
 for i in range(5):
 t = threading.Thread(target=critical_section_example, args=(i,))
 threads.append(t)
 
 # Start all threads
 for t in threads:
 t.start()
 
 # Wait for all to complete
 for t in threads:
 t.join()
 
 print(f"\nFinal counter value: {shared_counter}")
 print(f"Expected value: 5")
 print(f"Mutual exclusion working: {shared_counter == 5}")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates the test-and-set concept for mutual exclusion. The Spinlock class uses a busy-waiting (spin) mechanism where threads continuously test the lock until it becomes available. The critical_section_example shows proper use of acquire/release around the critical section. In a real system, test_and_set would be a single atomic CPU instruction (like x86 'xchg' or ARM 'swp'), but Python's GIL allows us to simulate this behavior.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for acquire in best case, O(n) worst case where n is number of waiting processes

examples.title: C: Test-and-Set and Compare-and-Swap Implementation
examples.description: C implementation showing atomic hardware instructions for mutual exclusion and lock-free programming
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates three key hardware synchronization approaches: 1) test_and_set for basic mutual exclusion using spinlocks, 2) compare_and_swap for more flexible conditional updates, and 3) a lock-free counter that uses CAS in a retry loop. The test_implementation function creates multiple threads that all increment a shared counter, verifying that mutual exclusion works correctly. In real systems, these would use compiler intrinsics like __sync_bool_compare_and_swap() or assembly instructions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized for lock acquisition; worst case unbounded for spinlock due to scheduling
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for lock structures, O(n) for n threads
